course.title: Turkish for Beginners
course.description: Everyday Turkish: greetings, numbers, and food, with the vowel harmony and case rules that make the language click.

lessons.title: Selamlaşma (Greetings)
lessons.content: Turkish greetings change with the time of day far more than English ones do. Note that 'günaydın' is strictly a morning word — using it at 3pm marks you instantly as a learner.
lessons.grammar_note: Turkish is agglutinative: suffixes stack onto a root in a fixed order. 'Teşekkür ederim' is literally 'thanks I-do' — 'ederim' is 'etmek' (to do) + first-person present. You'll meet this compound-verb pattern constantly.
lessons.cultural_note: 'Hoşça kal' (stay well) is said by the person leaving; the one staying says 'güle güle' (go smiling). Using the wrong one is a small but very audible slip.

questions.question_text: What is the English translation of 'merhaba'?
Complete the phrase for 'thank you': ___ ederim
Put the words in order to wish someone a good night.

lessons.title: Sayılar (Numbers)
lessons.content: Turkish numbers are fully regular once you know 1–10: there are no irregular teens like English 'eleven' or 'twelve'. Eleven is simply 'on bir' (ten one).
lessons.grammar_note: After a number, Turkish nouns stay singular: 'beş elma' (five apple), never 'beş elmalar'. The number already carries the plurality, so the -lar/-ler suffix would be redundant.
lessons.cultural_note: Turkish counts on fingers starting from the thumb, and 'çeyrek' (a quarter) shows up constantly in tea culture and time-telling alike.

questions.question_text: What is the English translation of 'üç'?
How do you say 'eleven' in Turkish?

lessons.title: Yemek (Food)
lessons.content: Food vocabulary is where Turkish vowel harmony becomes unavoidable: suffixes shift their vowels to match the word they attach to.
lessons.grammar_note: The accusative suffix harmonises four ways: ekmeği, suyu, elmayı, mısırı. The vowel you hear is dictated by the last vowel of the noun — this is vowel harmony doing its job.
lessons.cultural_note: Offering food is close to obligatory in Turkish hospitality, and refusing outright can read as cold. 'Afiyet olsun' (may it be healthy) is said both before and after eating.

questions.question_text: What is the English translation of 'ekmek'?
Put the words in order: 'I would like a bottle of water.'
